report_metadata.fund_name: Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP
report_metadata.fund_letterupdate: The fourth quarter of 2023 continued to reflect a challenging global venture capital environment, with venture funding in Africa falling 46% year-over-year to $3.5 billion. Growth-stage investments were particularly affected, and the absence of large rounds limited exit opportunities for early-stage investors, leading to several startup failures and down rounds. Despite the downturn, the fund's portfolio showed resilience, with some companies like Moniepoint and Poa demonstrating extraordinary growth in local currencies. The fund's valuations remained steady, with the portfolio's fair value at $125.3 million, representing a 1.68x multiple on invested capital. The fund completed two new investments in the quarter, a $5 million investment in KOKO Networks and a $1.5 million follow-on in mPharma.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: mPharma seeks to provide better access to affordable primary healthcare for African consumers. They do so by owning and operating their own pharmacies, by franchising to third party phamacists, and by providing a health management programme with access to virtual doctors for customers (mutti).
portfolio_companies.investment_update: mPharma's Q4 was focused on rightsizing the business after securing a last-minute internal round. The company laid off over 300 staff, shut down its wholesale and diagnostics units, and rationalized its franchise portfolio, resulting in a 62% decline in Q4 revenues. However, these measures improved gross margins significantly and reduced monthly cash burn, positioning the company to target positive EBITDA and cash flow in 2024.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: MoniePoint provides digital financial and banking services to underserved consumers and entrepreneurs using an offline distribution network of agents. Through the network, customers can open bank accounts, access cash-in and cash-out services, pay bills, save, and eventually invest.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Moniepoint delivered a strong quarter, with FY2023 revenues reaching $262.9 million, a 66% increase from the previous year. The company is making significant progress on its Series C fundraising, with the round size expected to be over $150 million. Key executive hires, including a new CFO for its microfinance bank, are set to strengthen the company's financial leadership.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: TradeDepot brings informal retailers and FMCGs onto a single platform that ensures retailers have consistent access to the inventory and financial services required to build their businesses and provides transparency to manufacturers through the entire supply chain. The company offers a wide range of SKUs, working capital and logistics services to these retailers.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: TradeDepot had a relatively flat quarter with sales of $38.6 million. The company continues to focus on achieving profitability by Q2 2024 through new initiatives, including exporting products to the UK to earn foreign currency and expanding its operations in Ghana. Despite the turbulent B2B eCommerce market, TradeDepot maintains over 12 months of cash runway.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Elephant is a health technology company focused on transforming primary healthcare for the mass low-income market in emerging economies. The company has built and deployed a lightweight cloud-based operating system that connects all parts of the health service – from patient records, to clinical support, stock management, billing, and reporting.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Elephant experienced a mixed quarter, with stakeholder misalignment delaying revenue realization from contracts in Anambra State and Abuja. However, the company successfully signed and began implementing a patient-pay contract in Niger State. Facing a cash shortage, the company secured additional funding from an existing investor and an approved investment from NVAF II to extend its runway to October, providing time to validate its patient-pay model.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Metro Africa Xpress (MAX) leverages technology to provide on-demand mobility services to customers in West Africa while giving riders and drivers an opportunity to own an asset and have a sustainable livelihood. MAX offers commuters a high quality, affordable, app-based ride-hailing service.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: MAX delivered a solid quarter despite the Naira's devaluation constraining top-line growth, with revenues remaining flat QoQ at $5.6m. The company achieved its highest number of new quarterly vehicle activations and increased its average collection rate to over 90%. MAX is also advancing M&A discussions with PikiPiki, which would expand its footprint into Togo and Benin.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Turaco sells simple and affordable insurance products through distribution partnerships with companies who have access to low-income customers, staff, suppliers or distributors at scale. Turaco differentiates itself from traditional insurance brokers by specifically designing and selling insurance products for low-income consumers, as well as managing administration and claims on behalf of underwriters.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Turaco's Q4 performance reflected ongoing challenges, with Gross Written Premiums (GWPs) of $639k, down 18% from Q3, impacted by partners' funding constraints and currency depreciation. Despite the tough quarter, the company grew its full-year GWPs by 13% to $2.6m. Turaco is now prioritizing deepening key existing partnerships like M-KOPA and advancing new ones with major players like Safaricom and Equity Bank.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: KOKO is a climate technology company harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across markets in the Global South. KOKO delivers its bioethanol "KOKO Fuel" through over 2,800 KOKO points (fuel ATMs) which are positioned in small shops at the heart of low-income communities.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: KOKO Networks was added as the 12th company in the fund's portfolio following a $5 million investment in its $46.4 million equity round. In Q4, KOKO successfully launched its services in Rwanda, aiming to reach 1 million households there within five years. The company also secured a $30 million first close of its carbon receivables financing debt facility.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Sure Chill has developed a unique cooling technology enabling refrigerators to maintain a constant temperature without constant power supply. The technology was first developed for use in the medical cold chain as vaccine refrigerators and is now being adapted for domestic fridges in off-grid and weak-grid households in Africa.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Sure Chill faced a challenging quarter as a delay in the Global Vaccine Alliance (GAVI) program caused its medical refrigerator sales to plummet by 80% versus Q3, down to $303k. The company is experiencing a cash shortage and has a runway only until March 2024, pending the close of a delayed $4m debt facility. It is now urgently seeking an additional $3m in equity from new and existing investors.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Poa provides low cost internet to individuals and small businesses in low-income neighborhoods in East Africa. Poa services are delivered through a Wi-Fi network that delivers both public internet access as well as internet to the home.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Poa achieved remarkable customer growth, adding nearly 10,000 new subscribers in Q4, which represents 25% of its total customer base. However, the significant devaluation of the Kenyan shilling muted this growth in dollar terms, with annual USD revenues rising by 48% despite a near doubling of customers. The company is now focused on cost-saving measures and aims to double its customer base again in 2024 to achieve EBITDA positivity in Kenya.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: BasiGo is transforming Africa's public bus transport sector by making it affordable and convenient to own and operate electric buses. The company sources high quality electric buses, provides a Pay-as-you-Drive financing model for bus owners, overnight charging, service and maintenance, and will eventually assemble the buses locally.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: BasiGo finished 2023 strongly with several key achievements in Q4, including the launch of its pilot program in Rwanda and the commencement of local bus assembly in Nairobi. The company grew its reservation list to 450 paid reservations in Kenya. On the fundraising front, BasiGo closed a $3m SAFE investment from CFAO and received a term sheet from Africa50 to lead its $15m Series A round.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Ignitia is a tropical weather forecasting company providing forecasts and productivity messages to over 2 million smallholder farmers across West Africa, Brazil, and East Africa. The company has developed a proprietary weather forecasting model that predicts tropical weather patterns down to a hyperlocal range with twice the accuracy of global models.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Ignitia had a challenging quarter as management was distracted by a leadership transition, which also delayed fundraising efforts. Despite this, the company grew Q4 revenue by 42% year-over-year to $556k and signed its first major commercial contract in Brazil with telecom giant Claro. The focus for Q1 2024 is on the commercial launch in Brazil and building traction for new products.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: iProcure is an agricultural supply chain company focused on farm supply innovation in Kenya. The company connects agricultural input suppliers with local retailers.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: iProcure is facing an existential crisis due to mounting debt, a depleted cash runway, and the failure to attract new equity capital. Despite drastic cost-cutting measures and a pivot in strategy, an administration process appears inevitable. The company is currently in discussions with potential acquirers, including Safaricom's Digifarm and Apollo Agriculture, for a partial or full sale of its operations.

<::NOVASTAR logo with a star symbol: figure::> Novastar Ventures Limited – Strictly Private & Confidential 3 Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP 2. MANAGER'S LETTER We are pleased to present the Q4 2023 Quarterly Report for Novastar Ventures East Africa Fund II LP ("the Fund"). # Challenging Market Conditions Our markets continued to mirror the dismal performance of VC globally. 2023 venture funding in Africa was $3.5 billion, falling 46% from last year, with debt comprising 35% of the total.¹ Growth stage investments were hardest hit. There was only one equity deal worth more than $100m announced in 2023 vs. seven such mega-deals the previous year. This isn't surprising since many of the large growth rounds raised in previous years were led by global funds with no dedicated Africa mandates. Those investors largely vanished from the Africa ecosystem to focus on their home markets amidst the broader VC contraction. The absence of large growth rounds limited secondary exit opportunities for earlier-stage investors. The contraction in funding together with steep rises in cost of living, precipitous local currency devaluations, and company-specific issues drove more than 15 African tech startups out of business during the year, including several that had raised more than $25m of external capital like Dash, 54gene, and Sendy. Other well-known venture-backed firms fought to survive via large layoffs and down rounds (e.g., Copia, MarketForce, and Twiga). The most vulnerable companies in this difficult environment are those that remain far from operational profitability with an inelastic cost structure. The market has favored capital efficient businesses that don't burn a lot of cash searching for product-market and business-model fit. Companies that need to build vertically integrated business models to reliably deliver their product or service (not unusual in our markets where so many value chains are broken) are finding the current environment extremely challenging. The silver lining among the dark clouds is best seen by taking a step back and up. Africa VC funding levels in 2023 exceeded those prior to the big surge in 2021-22 by a wide margin (47% higher than 2019 and 69% higher than 2020). Up 14x since 2015, the growth and development of the venture ecosystem in Africa is unambiguous despite the turmoil of the last 18 months. Africa's fundamental growth story means there is no shortage of large-scale opportunities, and economic downturns have a history of spawning battle-hardened entrepreneurs building businesses that solve real-world problems for everyday people. Companies that weather the storm will be leaner and wiser. They will see opportunities to acquire distressed assets, consolidate their position, access new markets, and grow. Meanwhile, investors committed to the region will find exciting, transformative investment opportunities at better prices. ## Portfolio Performance Many of our portfolio companies have performed remarkably well in such a difficult context. For example: * **Moniepoint** continued to buck the trend, growing revenues by 66% over last year in dollars to $262.9m. In the context of the Naira in near free fall, this is gravity-defying performance. The 1 https://partechpartners.com/news/presenting-the-2023-partech-africa-report-a-rough-year-for-the-ecosystem <::NOVASTAR logo with a stylized star icon to the top right of the 'R'::> Novastar Ventures Limited - Strictly Private & Confidential 4 Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP Series C financing round is back on track after a competing offer resulted in an increase in both valuation and round size. We expect first close legals to be signed by the end of February and plan to exit part of our stake in the company. * **BasiGo** successfully launched a 2-bus pilot in Rwanda, expanded its list of paid reservations to 450 and commenced local bus assembly in Nairobi, secured debt financing and a term sheet from Africa50 for its Series A equity round later this year. * **Poa** signed up nearly 10,000 new customers in the quarter – 25% of its total customer base in just 3 months – and grew its dollar revenues by 48% over last year (80% in Kenyan shillings). That is extraordinary growth into the headwinds of a cost-of-living crisis and slumping local currency in Kenya. * **MAX** activated more vehicles in Q4 than ever before (4,769) while improving collection rates and paved the way for acquisitions that will see it expand from 3 to 5 country markets. * **mPharma** continued to demonstrate remarkable resilience, completing its restructure and pivot to profitability. While management sacrificed revenue growth in the process, gross margins jumped from 28% to 41%, providing a strong platform for sustainable growth to profitability in 2024. Some in our portfolio have not fared so well in the storm. iProcure's restructure and pivot is unfortunately proving too little and too late. Sadly, that business looks like the first casualty in this Fund. Meanwhile, SureChill and Elephant Healthcare both face short runways to prove their viability, though Elephant seems to have a clearer path to scale if they can prove their patient-pay business model in Q1 this year. ## Investment Activity We completed two investments at the beginning of the quarter: * $5m into KOKO Networks $46.4m growth equity round (inclusive of convertible notes worth $21m). KOKO completes the Fund's portfolio and is introduced more fully later in the report. * $1.5m into mPharma's $13.7m Series C-2 led by existing investor Unbound ($5m) with $3m from new investor French pharmaceutical giant Sanofi. The Investment Committee (IC) approved $1m into Poa's $6m rights issue led by Africa50. In January, the IC approved up to £400k into Elephant's Series A-3, tranched in two equal amounts based on the achievement of commercial milestones.

_Valuations_ Valuations of the on-going portfolio were up a modest $758k over last quarter. While that sounds lackluster on first hearing, it is remarkable that valuations held so steady in the teeth of such adverse market conditions. We increased the valuation of our holding in Moniepoint from $60.8m to $64.5m to reflect the blended price (weighted average of primary and secondary price) agreed between the company and its lead <::NOVASTAR logo with a star graphic::> Novastar Ventures Limited – Strictly Private & Confidential 5 Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP investor DPI. Africinvest, Lightrock, IFC, BII and Proparco are all expected to participate in this financing round providing further validation of the enterprise value. Inevitably, steep FX devaluations in our core markets yielding slower growth in dollars affected all our companies. Slower top line growth and the softening of comparable valuations prompted the following three downward adjustments in the valuations of our holdings: * **Turaco** from $5.9m to $3.5m based primarily on market comparables rather than company performance. * **TradeDepot** from $12.8m to $11m to reflect slower growth in dollar revenues hit by FX and the general economic conditions in Nigeria. * **Ignitia** from $1.4m to $1.1m based on reduced comparable revenue mutliples reflecting the tough current market conditions. With the two investments made in the quarter, the fair value of the portfolio stands at $125.3m, representing a 1.68x multiple on invested capital (MOIC) and 25% gross IRR (Internal Rate of Return). While we are disappointed to lose iProcure, the Fund has proven to be remarkably resilient in very challenging circumstances. Portfolio Company Developments The financial and operational performance of each of our portfolio companies is contained in the Portfolio Company pages later in the report. The following paragraphs summarize the most important developments in the portfolio during the quarter. mPharma mPharma's Q4 was all about rightsizing the business after a hasty internal round was pulled together at the last minute in Q3 to rescue the business. Company-wide layoffs in the end affected over 300 staff, the wholesale and diagnostics business units were shut down and the franchise portfolio rationalized. By December, monthly cash burn had been reduced by $208k to $380k as the company worked towards a 2024 target of positive EBITDA and positive cash flow. Nevertheless, the effects were a 62% decline in Q4 revenues to $4.4m and a 39% decline in the annual revenues to $18.4m (from $30.3m in 2022) - not helped by the Naira's collapse against the US dollar. Despite this, there were significant wins in three of the retail channels where the company's own stores reached EBITDA positive (excluding central costs) for the second consecutive quarter in Nigeria, Kenya, and Zambia on substantially improved gross margins. M Moniepoint Moniepoint had a strong quarter with revenues reaching $262.9m for FY2023, a 66% increase on FY2022 revenues. The company finalized its microfinance bank CFO hire, bringing in the former CFO of Stanbic Bank Nigeria. In addition, they have made significant steps towards securing a Global CFO with the identified candidate currently in advanced negotiations. These key hires will substantially transform the company's financial leadership, putting the company in its strongest financial position yet. This quarter, the company put on hold its plans to acquire Kopo Kopo as it awaits a decision from the Central Bank of Kenya on its acquisition of a microfinance bank in Kenya. The Series C continues to progress with legals expected to complete by end of February. AfricInvest have joined the consortium with the final round size expected to be north of $150m. <::NOVASTAR logo with a star icon::> Novastar Ventures Limited – Strictly Private & Confidential 6 Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP TradeDepot TradeDepot had a relatively flat quarter with sales reaching $38.6m versus $37.7m in Q3 2023. Gross margins also remained flat throughout the period. The company continues to work on reaching profitability by Q2 2024 with new initiatives launching this year including: exporting products to the UK and earning income in British Pounds; securing additional exclusive distribution contracts with international suppliers; importing white label beverages into Nigeria and Ghana; investing in growth in Ghana. While the B2B eCommerce industry faced a lot of turbulence in 2023, we are delighted that TradeDepot not only has over 12 months of runway but is also poised to reach profitability within this calendar year before kickstarting growth. elephant Elephant had a mixed quarter. Stakeholder misalignment disrupted the implementation of contracts in Anambra State and Federal Capital Territory Abuja causing delays to revenue realization. However, the team banked important lessons from the challenges encountered and, in December, signed and began implementing a patient-pay contract in Niger State after a joint sale to the Governor and the Health Commissioner that ensured full stakeholder alignment. Delayed revenues from Anambra and FCT Abuja plus a smaller than anticipated capital raise (£1.95m versus a targeted £2.5 - £3.0m) meant that Elephant needed cash at the end of December. Existing investor, Manta Ray, stepped up to invest £200k but will not continue to fund the business alone. In January, the NVAF II investment committee approved an investment of up to £400k to unlock an additional £800k from Manta Ray. This capital, together with Elephant's annual R&D tax credit (c. £225k) and a soft loan from DFC now in due diligence (£800k), provides the company with runway to October and time to hit key patient-pay targets in Niger State that will demonstrate that its patient-pay revenue model is scalable. M max MAX had a solid quarter despite the continued devaluation of the Naira constraining top-line growth; revenues were flat QoQ at $5.6m. The unstable macro-economic environment in Nigeria means it has been hard to secure an external lead for its planned $15-30m pre-Series C equity round. The debt raise has also been lagging although MAX is now in due diligence with Untapped ($10m) and AHL ($10m). Key achievements in the quarter included activating 4,769 new vehicles - the highest number of new quarterly activations all year - and increasing the average collection rate to over 90% after implementing a more flexible approach to restructuring driver contracts which has minimised defaults and optimised retention. MAX also completed due diligence on an M&A target, Pikipiki - an EV bike financing business operating in Togo and Benin - which, if completed, would extend MAX's geographic footprint to five countries. tu'aco Turaco's Q4 2023 reflected both the challenges and opportunities it faced through the year. The constrained funding environment left many of its startup partners focusing on survival and deprioritizing growth. Tugende, Turaco's largest partner by revenue, was in survival mode with no capacity to drive growth for itself or Turaco. The Airtel partnership has helped Turaco grow its active policies more than 2x from 160k to 415k, but integration has been slow, and Airtel's customers have been difficult to convert to paid subscriptions. With these headwinds compounded by depreciating currencies, Turaco closed out a tough Q4 2023 with Gross Written Premiums (GWPs) of $639k which were 18% below Q3 performance. Turaco still demonstrated resilience by closing FY2023 with growth as GWPs were 13% up from FY 2022 at $2.6m. Anne-Elise Francis, Turaco's Head of Growth transitioned out of the business in Q4 as the growth <::Logo: NOVASTAR with a star graphic::> Novastar Ventures Limited - Strictly Private & Confidential 7 Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP model shifted to being led by the in-country teams. The board mandated management to prioritize deepening existing partnerships such as M-KOPA and One Acre Fund and to focus on catalytic new partnerships such as Safaricom, Palm Pay and Equity Bank. Turaco advanced all these partnerships materially in Q4 with M-KOPA launching a new product with Turaco which has the potential to reach 2,000 new customers a day in Kenya and Safaricom taking a proposal for a JV with Turaco to its board late in Q4. poa! internet Poa ended 2023 with 39k customers, almost a doubling from the 20k base at the end of 2022 – a remarkable achievement given the cost-of-living squeeze. Indeed, Poa signed up 9,917 new customers during Q4 – representing 25% of their total customer base at the year end, and a 45% increase over the new customers signed up during Q3. This acceleration in Q4 is coming from the new networks now open in Nakuru and Mombasa compounding the growth in the existing networks in Nairobi. Sadly, the doubling of the customer base in the year has not led to a corresponding growth in USD denominated revenues - which rose by 48% from $2.1m in 2022 to $3.2m in 2023. If measured in Kenyan shillings, the revenues grew 80%, during the year. The FX slide has hit Poa particularly badly because all its equipment costs are denominated in hard currency, so even as they make improvements to operations, the lifetime value (LTV) of a customer is going backwards when denominated in USD – from over $80 in 2022 to $56 in 2023. Without the weakening of the Kenyan shilling, LTV would have increased from $80 to over $110 over the last year. The company is working hard to combat the squeeze and have already renegotiated bandwidth prices into Shillings, reduced customer acquisition costs and are working on other cost reductions tied to high density installations in Nairobi. In 2024 they see a path to doubling customer numbers again to 80k and becoming EBITDA positive in Kenya, launching networks into new cities elsewhere in the country, as well as opening their first network outside the country. BASIGO) BasiGo ended 2023 on a high, with a number of successes in Q4: the Rwandan pilot was launched and by the time of publishing this quarterly report, 2 buses have already operated for 50 days in Kigali; the larger 36-seat 'Kubwa' pilot buses have proven successful in Nairobi, with every pilot operator requesting to keep the buses; the reservation list in Kenya grew to 450 paid reservations; assembly of two completely knocked down kit ("CKD") buses started at Kenya Vehicle Manufacturers. On the fundraising front, CFAO, Toyoto Tsusho's operating entity in East Africa approved a $3m participation in the SAFE at a $30m post-money valuation cap, the $5m BII debt facility was closed and Africa50 received IC approval for $7m participation in the Series A at a pre money valuation of $30m. The Africa50 term sheet followed in January 2024 and is under negotiation. Closing the $15m Series A is now a clear priority as it is required to scale up the number of buses on the road in Nairobi and Kigali to meet increasing demand from bus operators. SureChill. Sure Chill had a difficult quarter. Despite a $2.5m equity raise in Q3, the company is running out of cash after the Global Vaccine Alliance (GAVI) delayed its vaccine programme to 2025 causing sales of the company's medical vaccine refrigerator to plummet; Q4 sales fell to just $303k, an 80% drop versus Q3. Furthermore, additional delays to closing a $4m working capital facility with Triodos and Oiko Credit have prevented Sure Chill from executing its Home and Small Business plan leaving this segment in stasis. Thankfully, this debt facility is now on the verge of being signed. These challenges mean the company now needs an additional $3m of equity to get to breakeven in 2025 and, assuming the debt lands, only has runway until March 2024. Existing investors, Gaia Impact Fund and Chroma Impact Investment, are prepared to bridge the company to a new equity investor if a term sheet is secured before then. <::NOVASTAR logo with a stylized star icon to the top right of the 'R'::> Novastar Ventures Limited – Strictly Private & Confidential 8 Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP Discussions are ongoing with interested new investors including EDFI (conducting on-side due diligence in February), Sanofi Impact (going to first IC screening on 16th February), Aquaspark (targeting IC in April) and All-On. <::logo: iProcure iProcure FARM SUPPLY INNOVATION The logo features a stylized 'i' in black, forming the first part of a play icon, followed by a green triangular shape completing the play icon.::> iProcure is facing an existential crisis. This is despite implementing drastic cost-cutting measures, negotiating with creditors to restructure outstanding debt, seeking additional equity capital, and pivoting to focus on low-volume, high-margin products. Regrettably, the company is staring into the abyss with an administration process looking inevitable because of mounting debt obligations, depleted cash runway, and failure to attract critical equity capital. However, iProcure is in discussions with Safaricom's Digifarm, Apollo Agriculture and Shamba Pride regarding the potential partial or full total acquisition of the company's operations. ignitia TROPICAL WEATHER FORECASTING Ignitia had a difficult quarter, primarily due to management being distracted by challenges arising from its leadership transition. The challenges also led to the equity capital raise being put on hold while the company's board focused on helping to resolve the issues. Despite the distraction, management continued to push forward on key initiatives and late in the period the company signed its first major commercial contract in Brazil with Claro, one of the largest telecommunications companies globally with Latin America as its main territory. Ignitia's Q4 revenue was $556k, which was 48% below budget but represented 42% growth from the same period in 2022. Similarly, FY2023 revenue from operations was $2.4m representing 28% growth from FY2022 (excluding accounting adjustments of $730k). The company signed new contracts worth over $1.4m in 2023 compared with new contracts of $400k in 2022. Following the upgrades to its science, product, distribution and payment infrastructure the company also revamped its product pipeline and started releasing new products including nowcast (instant weather alerts) and extreme weather alerts which are expected to further accelerate its growth in 2024. <::logo: KOKO KOKO The logo features a stylized letter "K" in a bright blue color, with rounded edges and an arrow-like shape within its design.::> We are excited to add KOKO Networks as the 12th company in the NVAF II portfolio. KOKO delivers its bioethanol "KOKO Fuel" through over 2,800 KOKO points (fuel ATMs) which are positioned in small shops at the heart of low-income communities. By switching from charcoal cooking to a sustainable biofuel, each household subscriber on average reduces CO2e emissions by over 5 tonnes per annum, generating carbon credits. KOKO monetises these carbon credits and uses the proceeds to discount the retail price of its cookstove and fuel, driving adoption and undercutting the polluting competition. KOKO also achieved a $30m first close of its carbon receivables financing debt facility led by Rand Merchant Bank. Organisational Development NVIII Africa People + Planet Fund We completed a 'dry closing' of our Africa People & Planet Fund III just prior to year-end, signing all documentation with Norfund who have partnered with us in our two previous funds. We have agreed core commercial terms with DFC (the US development bank) and SBI Investments, while Mitsubishi and MOL each cleared final investment committees (IC) in January. We await final approvals from two other institutional investors who have completed due diligence, after which we can proceed to a first closing of the Fund with c. $78m of our targeted $150m to $200m final fund size. If you are interested in learning more about NV III, please let us know. We would be happy to provide more details. BlueMark Independent Impact Verification We engaged BlueMark, a leading provider of impact verification services for investors and companies, to conduct an independent verification of our approach to social and environmental value. BlueMark's verification methodologies draw on a range of industry standards, frameworks, and regulations, including the Impact Management Project, the Operating Principles for Impact Management, the Principles for Responsible Investment, SDG Impact, and the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation. At the time of writing, BlueMark is completing its analysis of our impact management framework and procedures to issue its final report, which we will share with you along with the agreed actions to improve our processes. # Team News We are delighted to announce Abel's promotion to Investment Director. Abel joined us in 2019 as an Investment Associate after stints as a Credit Research Analyst at Bloomberg in New York, and an agritech founder and business leader in Kenya. Abel seems to know everyone in our ecosystem (and everyone knows him!) and is a proven entrepreneur talent-spotter and effective investment manager. Please join us in congratulating him. We were sorry to say 'good-bye' to two of our team who seized promotion opportunities at the beginning of this year. Thea has taken the next step in her career taking a role as a client relationship manager in a boutique asset management firm in London, while Max moved to mobile money payments firm PawaPay as their Group Financial Controller. We miss them both and wish them well in their new endeavors. On the flip side, we are excited to be adding a talented Ugandan woman as Head of Sustainability who will oversee ESG at Novastar and bring her considerable operational experience to our portfolio companies. We will introduce her more fully next quarter as she is serving an extended notice period at one of our portfolio companies so cannot join us full time until May. Welcome BlueEarth Capital In January, British International Investment (BII), the UK's development finance institution, consummated the sale of 40% of its stake in NVAF II to BlueEarth Capital, the Swiss-based private impact investor as part of a deal that included BII's stakes in two other funds across Africa and Asia. Portfolio Company Reports - BasiGo Cont'd ### Key Performance Indicators 1. **Launch the pilot in Rwanda:** two buses were launched in Kigali and as at the end of January 2024, BasiGo has carried more than 80k passengers there. Two additional buses start operating in February. Technical performance is exceeding expectations (likely due to better roads in Rwanda) but mileage per bus is lower than expected. BasiGo is investigating why this is the case. The Ministry of Insfrastructure are keen for BasiGo to order more buses, and there is urgency to do this before the next election season in Q3. Basigo is in discussions with the Rwandan Green Fund for a local currency debt facility, as well as the Bank of Kigali. 2. **Locally assemble two imported CKD kits with Kenya Vehicle Manufacturers:** 2 buses were assembled at KVM and as at the end of January 2024, the buses were completed and entered the testing and quality control phase. These buses took c2,000 labour hours and 2 months to complete. The next batch (due at the end of February) is estimated to take half the time. Missing parts from CHTC was one of the primary challenges that led to delays. Members of the BasiGo engineering and assembly team are with CHTC in China providing feedback and establishing a clear plan for each step of the assembly process 3. **Close the $5M unsecured debt facility from BII:** The facility closed in November 2023 and DFC kicked off their process for an additional $10m on the same terms as BII. 4. **Close the $2M seed extension and progress Series A fundraising:** With the CFAO approval for a $3m investment in the SAFE, the seed extension is now closed. Mobility54, an existing SAFE investor, is the CVC arm of CFAO (Toyota Tsusho's operating entity in Kenya). Focus is now on negotiating the Africa50 lead term sheet for the $15m Series A and getting the Series A closed as fast as possible. Key priorities for Q1 2024 include: 1. Close the Series A raise 2. Raise debt for Rwanda 3. Reduce CKD assembly time Challenges With the growing demand in Kenya & Rwanda, a key challenge is raising equity & debt fast enough to meet the demand. If the e-buses don't come online soon, there is a risk that interested operators buy diesel buses instead. Financial Performance

Business DescriptionKOKO is a climate technology company harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across emerging markets. This "KOKO Fuel" is delivered through an asset-light, scalable and proprietary distribution model. KOKO currently serve customers in Kenya and Rwanda.
Investment Background Location of head office Nairobi, Kenya Fund equity percentage 0.91% Fund cost $5,000,000 Equity $5,000,000 Equity value of company $551,500,000 Fund equity value $5,000,000 Equity $5,000,000 Date of last investment: Oct-23 Stage of initial investment: Series D Next equity financing: n/a Date of valuation: Dec-23 Valuation method: Valuation based on last equity round closed in Q4 2023 Investment manager DM Investment team DM, SS, AC Board representation n/a Fund's role in initial investment: Participated in $25.7m equity round (No lead investor) Investor Syndicate: Alder Tree Investments, Chroma Impact Investment and Seceral family offices and HNWIs Domicile Mauritius 49 <::NOVASTAR logo with a star icon : figure::> VI. Portfolio Company Reports - KoKo Cont'd Investment Rationale KOKO is harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across markets in the Global South. Over 1 million hectares of African tropical forests are destroyed annually to produce charcoal, leading to 1 billion tons of carbon emissions yearly (2% of global emissions). Meanwhile, 600,000 people die in Africa annually from indoor air pollution-related causes. Due to no convenient and affordable clean cooking fuels, people living in Africa's rapidly growing cities have no choice but to continue using charcoal which is readily available and can be bought in small, affordable quantities. KOKO delivers clean, safe, affordable bioethanol cooking fuel - "KOKO Fuel" - through an asset-light, scalable and proprietary distribution model. KOKO uses the proceeds from carbon credits to discount the retail price of its cookstove and fuel, driving adoption and undercutting the competing dirty cooking fuels. When applied like this, carbon credits act as a non-government economic subsidy channeled into the hands of everyday consumers in low-income communities. Greg Murray, co-founder and CEO, has been a pioneer in the African carbon space, securing KOKO credits' access to the Korean Emissions Trading Scheme and positioning KOKO to enter other compliance carbon markets. KOKO's economic model is highly attractive due its recurring carbon revenues, its low cost of goods as governments have agreed to remove taxes on KOKO's hardware and fuel and low FX risk as 70% of KOKO's revenues are in hard currency from the Global North. Founded in 2014, the founders, Greg Murray and Sagun Saxena, have shown tremendous tenacity to build and scale what is, effectively, a new energy utility without government support or significant investment. The initial years were spent focusing on developing its end-to-end technology and securing critical partnerships to ensure fuel distribution and carbon market entry. In October 2019, KOKO launched commercial operations has since grown to serve over 1.1m households in Kenya, across 12 out of 14 urban centers. The company has recently launched operations in Rwanda. The agreement with the Rwandan Government secures a tax exemption on KOKO hardware and fuel and a government commitment to "corresponding adjustments" on all KOKO carbon credits which will enable them to be sold into compliance markets. KOKO plans to expand to 6 additional countries over the next three years. ## Social Impact KOKO's clean, safe and sustainable cooking solution generates the following social and environmental benefits: 1. Cost savings for low-income households – KOKO sell carbon credits to subsidise the price of cookstoves and fuel. As a result, KOKO's stoves are sold for $12 despite a cost of goods of $60 and its fuel is 50% cheaper than charcoal for the same energy delivered to the pot. To date, KOKO has transferred more than $100m of carbon value to more than 1.1m households in Kenya. 2. CO2e emission reductions as a result of moving away from charcoal (a huge cause of deforestation) and towards a cleaner, more sustainable cooking fuel. 3. No indoor air pollution from cooking which averts respiratory disease and deaths. 4. New, profitable business line for KOKO Point partners, typically female-owned or operated small shops, which sell KOKO cookstoves and fuel in ATMs. ## Exit Strategy KOKO is building a new, pan-African clean energy utility with a rapidly growing customer base, attractive unit economics and a strong, experienced team. Possible exit routes include an IPO, an acquisition by a global energy company (e.g. Shell or Total) or an acquisition by a trading conglomerate (e.g. Itochu, Vitol or Mitsubishi). These global players are under pressure to diversity their revenue sources away from fossil fuels and towards clean energy.

report_metadata.fund_name: Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP
report_metadata.fund_investment_region: East and West Africa (Primary countries Kenya and Nigeria)
report_metadata.fund_letterupdate: The Q4 2023 report highlights challenging market conditions in Africa's VC sector, with funding down 46% in 2023, yet exceeding pre-2021 levels. Despite this, several portfolio companies like Moniepoint, BasiGo, Poa, MAX, and mPharma showed remarkable resilience and growth. The fund made new investments in KOKO Networks and mPharma, while adjusting valuations for Turaco, TradeDepot, and Ignitia downwards. The letter also covers team developments and impact verification efforts.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: mPharma seeks to provide better access to affordable primary healthcare for African consumers. They do so by owning and operating their own pharmacies, by franchising to third party phamacists, and by providing a health management programme with access to virtual doctors for customers (mutti). mPharma currently operates in Nigeria, Ghana, Togo, Gabon, Zambia, Malawi, Kenya, Rwanda, Uganda and Ethiopia.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: mPharma's Q4 was all about rightsizing the business after a hasty internal round was pulled together at the last minute in Q3 to rescue the business. Company-wide layoffs in the end affected over 300 staff, the wholesale and diagnostics business units were shut down and the franchise portfolio rationalized. By December, monthly cash burn had been reduced by $208k to $380k as the company worked towards a 2024 target of positive EBITDA and positive cash flow. Nevertheless, the effects were a 62% decline in Q4 revenues to $4.4m and a 39% decline in the annual revenues to $18.4m (from $30.3m in 2022) - not helped by the Naira's collapse against the US dollar. Despite this, there were significant wins in three of the retail channels where the company's own stores reached EBITDA positive (excluding central costs) for the second consecutive quarter in Nigeria, Kenya, and Zambia on substantially improved gross margins. The focus for Q1 2024 is to rebuild the platform by bringing employees back to the office, enhancing wellness, introducing performance incentives, and improving customer experiences. They also aim to continue data-led optimization of inventory and improve procurement, inventory management, pricing, and billing. Challenges include large pharma exiting the market, significant FX devaluations, softening consumer spending, and brain drain.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: MoniePoint provides digital financial and banking services to underserved consumers and entrepreneurs using an offline distribution network of agents. Through the network, customers can open bank accounts, access cash-in and cash-out services, pay bills, save, and eventually invest. Today, the company operates the largest and fastest growing agent network and microbusiness banking platform in Nigeria.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Moniepoint had a strong quarter, growing revenues by 66% over last year to $262.9m, despite the Naira's devaluation. The Series C financing round is back on track with an increased valuation and round size, with first close legals expected by end of February. The company also finalized its microfinance bank CFO hire and made significant steps towards securing a Global CFO. They put on hold plans to acquire Kopo Kopo pending a decision from the Central Bank of Kenya. Priorities for Q1 2024 include closing the Series C first close, hiring a Global CFO, and building the Moniesend team. Challenges include the steep devaluation of the Nigerian Naira, which puts pressure on USD revenues and the Series C valuation.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: TradeDepot brings informal retailers and FMCGs onto a single platform that ensures retailers have consistent access to the inventory and financial services required to build their businesses and provides transparency to manufacturers through the entire supply chain. The company offers a wide range of SKUs, working capital and logistics services to these retailers.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: TradeDepot had a relatively flat quarter with sales reaching $38.6m versus $37.7m in Q3 2023, and gross margins remained flat. The company is working towards profitability by Q2 2024, launching initiatives like exporting products to the UK for FX earnings and securing additional exclusive distribution contracts. They are also investing in growth in Ghana. Priorities for Q1 2024 include launching UK operations and kickstarting growth in Ghana. The main challenge is the Nigerian Naira's devaluation, which constrains Gross Margin growth and keeps dollar revenues flat despite naira growth.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Elephant is a health technology company focused on transforming primary healthcare for the mass low-income market in emerging economies. The company has built and deployed a lightweight cloud-based operating system that connects all parts of the health service – from patient records, to clinical support, stock management, billing, and reporting. Elephant primarily serves government health systems in Kenya and Nigeria, with a pilot in Pakistan.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Elephant had a mixed quarter. Stakeholder misalignment disrupted contract implementation in Anambra State and Federal Capital Territory Abuja, delaying revenue. However, they signed and began implementing a patient-pay contract in Niger State with full stakeholder alignment. A smaller than targeted capital raise (£1.95m vs. £2.5-£3.0m) meant Elephant needed cash, with existing investor Manta Ray stepping up. The NVAF II investment committee approved up to £400k to unlock additional funding. Key achievements include signing a patient-pay contract in Niger State, closing £500k additional equity from Noso Capital, and progressing sales funnel discussions in Nigeria. Priorities for Q1 2024 include securing £1.2m in equity, achieving patient registration targets in Niger State, making progress in Anambra and FCT Abuja, clearing DFC's investment committee for an £800k soft loan, and launching HealthPay MVP in Kaduna State. Challenges include the capital raise shortfall and stakeholder misalignment in key regions.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Metro Africa Xpress (MAX) leverages technology to provide on-demand mobility services to customers in West Africa while giving riders and drivers an opportunity to own an asset and have a sustainable livelihood. MAX offers commuters a high quality, affordable, app-based ride-hailing service. The company uses a data-driven screening and onboarding process for its riders giving them access to an increased number of customers and access to an asset finance offering for the motorbikes.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: MAX had a solid quarter, with revenues flat QoQ at $5.6m due to Naira devaluation. They activated more vehicles in Q4 than ever before (4,769), improved collection rates to over 90%, and completed due diligence on M&A target Pikipiki to expand to 5 countries. They also launched operations in Abuja, Nigeria, and onboarded a new CFO and COO for West Africa. They are in due diligence for debt financing with Untapped and AHL. Priorities for Q1 2024 include closing additional debt, progressing the Pikipiki acquisition, and agreeing on a term sheet for the pre-Series C equity round. Challenges include the Naira's devaluation, difficulty securing a lead for the pre-Series C round, and struggles to raise sufficient debt capital for growth.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Turaco sells simple and affordable insurance products through distribution partnerships with companies who have access to low-income customers, staff, suppliers or distributors at scale and are willing to enable access to insurance to them. Turaco differentiates itself from traditional insurance brokers by specifically designing and selling insurance products for low-income consumers, as well as managing administration and claims on behalf of underwriters. Turaco currently has operations in Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria and Ghana.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Turaco's Q4 2023 reflected challenges and opportunities. Gross Written Premiums (GWPs) were $615k, 22% below Q3, due to macroeconomic challenges and funding constraints faced by partners. Momentum picked up late in the period with 'Autopay' mobile money payment with Airtel in Uganda. Following a strategy session, Turaco focused on deepening existing partnerships and closing new ones with established corporates, yielding encouraging results with M-KOPA and Safaricom. The company has initial approval for a $5-7m soft loan from DFC, and current runway is 12-15 months, aiming for 18 months to breakeven. Priorities for Q1 2024 include launching new products with M-KOPA, closing the Safaricom partnership, restructuring commercials with Airtel, and piloting AI capabilities for claims processing. Challenges include inefficient Airtel partnership, tapering Tugende partnership, and difficulty gaining traction in Nigeria due to macro-economic conditions.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: KOKO is a climate technology company harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across markets in the Global South. KOKO delivers its bioethanol "KOKO Fuel" through over 2,800 KOKO points (fuel ATMs) which are positioned in small shops at the heart of low-income communities. Customers buy the KOKO cookstove and then frequently refill the smart canister for as little as 30 Kenyan Shillings. By switching from charcoal cooking to a sustainable biofuel, each household subscriber on average reduces CO2e emissions by over 5 tonnes per annum, generating carbon credits. KOKO monetises these carbon credits and uses the proceeds to discount the retail price of its cookstove and fuel, driving adoption and undercutting the polluting competition. When applied like this, credits act as a powerful lever to deliver substantial cost savings to everyday consumers and to accelerate the adoption of planet-positive technologies.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP invested $5m into KOKO Networks' $46.4m growth equity round (inclusive of convertible notes worth $21m) in Q4 2023. KOKO Networks completes the Fund's portfolio. Since its commercial launch in 2019, KOKO has grown to serve over 1m households in Kenya, reducing CO2e emissions by over 8 million tonnes. In Q4, KOKO launched services in Rwanda, aiming to scale to 1 million Rwandan households in five years. The investment was priced at $525m pre-money. KOKO also achieved a $30m first close of its carbon receivables financing debt facility led by Rand Merchant Bank.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: SureChill is a company that develops and sells refrigeration technology, particularly for medical vaccine storage and home/small business use, focusing on off-grid solutions.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: SureChill had a difficult quarter. Despite a $2.5m equity raise in Q3, the company is running out of cash after GAVI delayed its vaccine program to 2025, causing Q4 sales to plummet to $303k (an 80% drop vs. Q3). Additional delays in closing a $4m working capital facility with Triodos and Oiko Credit prevented execution of its Home and Small Business plan. The debt facility is now on the verge of being signed. The company needs an additional $3m of equity to reach breakeven in 2025 and has runway only until March 2024, assuming the debt lands. Existing investors are prepared to bridge to a new equity investor if a term sheet is secured. Discussions are ongoing with interested new investors including EDFI, Sanofi Impact, Aquaspark, and All-On.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Poa provides affordable and reliable internet connectivity to customers, particularly in low-income areas.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Poa signed up nearly 10,000 new customers in Q4, representing 25% of its total customer base in just three months, and grew dollar revenues by 48% over last year (80% in Kenyan shillings). This extraordinary growth occurred despite a cost-of-living crisis and slumping local currency in Kenya. The doubling of the customer base in the year did not lead to a corresponding growth in USD denominated revenues, which rose by 48% from $2.1m in 2022 to $3.2m in 2023. The FX slide has hit Poa particularly badly because all its equipment costs are denominated in hard currency, causing the lifetime value (LTV) of a customer to decrease when denominated in USD. The company is working to combat this by renegotiating bandwidth prices into Shillings, reducing customer acquisition costs, and focusing on cost reductions tied to high-density installations. In 2024, they aim to double customer numbers again to 80k and become EBITDA positive in Kenya, launching networks in new cities and expanding outside the country.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: BasiGo is a company focused on electric mobility solutions, particularly electric buses, for public transport.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: BasiGo successfully launched a 2-bus pilot in Rwanda, expanded its list of paid reservations to 450, and commenced local bus assembly in Nairobi. The company secured debt financing and a term sheet from Africa50 for its Series A equity round later this year. CFAO approved a $3m participation in the SAFE at a $30m post-money valuation cap, the $5m BII debt facility was closed, and Africa50 received IC approval for $7m participation in the Series A at a pre-money valuation of $30m. The Africa50 term sheet followed in January 2024 and is under negotiation. Closing the $15m Series A is a clear priority to scale up the number of buses on the road in Nairobi and Kigali to meet increasing demand from bus operators.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Ignitia provides tropical weather forecasting services, particularly for farmers.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Ignitia had a difficult quarter due to management distraction from leadership transition challenges, which also put the equity capital raise on hold. Despite this, the company signed its first major commercial contract in Brazil with Claro. Ignitia's Q4 revenue was $556k, 48% below budget but representing 42% growth from Q4 2022. FY2023 revenue from operations was $2.4m, a 28% growth from FY2022 (excluding accounting adjustments). The company signed new contracts worth over $1.4m in 2023, significantly up from $400k in 2022. Following upgrades to its science, product, distribution, and payment infrastructure, Ignitia revamped its product pipeline and started releasing new products like nowcast and extreme weather alerts, expected to accelerate growth in 2024.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: iProcure is an agricultural supply chain innovation company, providing farm supplies.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: iProcure is facing an existential crisis despite drastic cost-cutting measures, debt restructuring negotiations, and a pivot to low-volume, high-margin products. The company is staring into administration due to mounting debt, depleted cash runway, and failure to attract critical equity capital. However, iProcure is in discussions with Safaricom's Digifarm, Apollo Agriculture, and Shamba Pride regarding potential partial or full acquisition of its operations.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Poa provides low cost internet to individuals and small businesses in low-income neighborhoods in East Africa. Poa services are delivered through a Wi-Fi network that delivers both public internet access as well as internet to the home, offering transparent pricing and unlimited data packages.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Customer numbers reached 39,000 by year-end, with monthly sign-ups peaking at 3,959, falling short of the 60,000 target due to cost of living crisis and launch delays in Nakuru and Mombasa. Poa has launched in Nakuru and Mombasa and plans international expansion in 2024. Time to customer breakeven was 26 months, but local currency devaluation impacted LTV. Challenges include currency decline and fundraising.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: BasiGo is a climate technology company transforming Africa's public bus transport sector by making electric buses affordable and convenient. They source high-quality electric buses, provide a Pay-as-you-Drive financing model, overnight charging, service, and maintenance, with plans for local assembly.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: BasiGo launched a pilot in Rwanda with two buses, carrying over 80k passengers, and plans for two more. Two buses were assembled locally in Kenya, with assembly time expected to halve for the next batch. A $5M debt facility from BII closed, and a $2M seed extension is closed with CFAO's $3M investment. The company is now focused on closing a $15M Series A round. Challenges include raising equity and debt fast enough to meet growing demand to prevent operators from buying diesel buses.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Sure Chill has developed a unique cooling technology that enables refrigerators to maintain a constant temperature without constant power supply. Initially used for medical cold chains, the technology is now being adapted for domestic fridges in off-grid and weak-grid households in Africa.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Q4 revenue was $303k, significantly below the $3.9m budget due to fundraising delays and a slowdown in GAVI medical fridge tenders. HSB sales are forecasted to grow 5.5x YoY to $1.1m in FY23/24. The senior team was strengthened with new hires. Legal documentation for a $4m working capital facility is drafted, with disbursement expected in Q1 2024. Discussions are progressing for an additional $3m equity to address cash flow issues. Challenges include severely delayed fundraising, GAVI program delays, and a short cash runway.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: Ignitia is a tropical weather forecasting company providing forecasts and productivity messages to over 2 million smallholder farmers in 8 countries across West Africa, Brazil, and East Africa. Their proprietary model predicts tropical weather patterns with high accuracy, offering an affordable, scalable, and useful service for farmers with limited literacy.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: Ignitia grew Q4 revenue by 42% to $556k compared to 2022, despite being 48% down from the previous quarter due to lack of capital injection. The company signed a major partnership with Claro in Brazil, contributing to $1.4m in new contracts in 2023 (250% up from 2022). Management has been distracted by leadership transition issues, delaying fundraising efforts.

portfolio_companies.investment_description: KOKO is a climate technology company that leverages global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel in emerging markets. Their 'KOKO Fuel' is delivered through an asset-light, scalable, and proprietary distribution model, currently serving customers in Kenya and Rwanda.
portfolio_companies.investment_update: KOKO is harnessing carbon markets to subsidize clean cooking fuel, addressing deforestation and indoor air pollution. Their economic model benefits from recurring carbon revenues, low cost of goods due to tax exemptions, and low FX risk. Founded in 2014, KOKO has scaled to serve over 1.1m households in Kenya and recently launched in Rwanda, with plans to expand to 6 additional countries. The company generates social and environmental benefits through cost savings for low-income households, CO2e emission reductions, reduced indoor air pollution, and new business lines for KOKO Point partners.

fund_name.value: Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP

fund_investment_region.value: East and West Africa (Primary countries Kenya and Nigeria)

fund_letterupdate.value: The Q4 2023 report highlights challenging market conditions in Africa's VC sector, with funding down 46% in 2023, yet exceeding pre-2021 levels. Despite this, several portfolio companies like Moniepoint, BasiGo, Poa, MAX, and mPharma showed remarkable resilience and growth. The fund made new investments in KOKO Networks and mPharma, while adjusting valuations for Turaco, TradeDepot, and Ignitia downwards. The letter also covers team developments and impact verification efforts.

investment_description.value: mPharma seeks to provide better access to affordable primary healthcare for African consumers. They do so by owning and operating their own pharmacies, by franchising to third party phamacists, and by providing a health management programme with access to virtual doctors for customers (mutti). mPharma currently operates in Nigeria, Ghana, Togo, Gabon, Zambia, Malawi, Kenya, Rwanda, Uganda and Ethiopia.

investment_update.value: mPharma's Q4 was all about rightsizing the business after a hasty internal round was pulled together at the last minute in Q3 to rescue the business. Company-wide layoffs in the end affected over 300 staff, the wholesale and diagnostics business units were shut down and the franchise portfolio rationalized. By December, monthly cash burn had been reduced by $208k to $380k as the company worked towards a 2024 target of positive EBITDA and positive cash flow. Nevertheless, the effects were a 62% decline in Q4 revenues to $4.4m and a 39% decline in the annual revenues to $18.4m (from $30.3m in 2022) - not helped by the Naira's collapse against the US dollar. Despite this, there were significant wins in three of the retail channels where the company's own stores reached EBITDA positive (excluding central costs) for the second consecutive quarter in Nigeria, Kenya, and Zambia on substantially improved gross margins. The focus for Q1 2024 is to rebuild the platform by bringing employees back to the office, enhancing wellness, introducing performance incentives, and improving customer experiences. They also aim to continue data-led optimization of inventory and improve procurement, inventory management, pricing, and billing. Challenges include large pharma exiting the market, significant FX devaluations, softening consumer spending, and brain drain.

investment_description.value: MoniePoint provides digital financial and banking services to underserved consumers and entrepreneurs using an offline distribution network of agents. Through the network, customers can open bank accounts, access cash-in and cash-out services, pay bills, save, and eventually invest. Today, the company operates the largest and fastest growing agent network and microbusiness banking platform in Nigeria.

investment_update.value: Moniepoint had a strong quarter, growing revenues by 66% over last year to $262.9m, despite the Naira's devaluation. The Series C financing round is back on track with an increased valuation and round size, with first close legals expected by end of February. The company also finalized its microfinance bank CFO hire and made significant steps towards securing a Global CFO. They put on hold plans to acquire Kopo Kopo pending a decision from the Central Bank of Kenya. Priorities for Q1 2024 include closing the Series C first close, hiring a Global CFO, and building the Moniesend team. Challenges include the steep devaluation of the Nigerian Naira, which puts pressure on USD revenues and the Series C valuation.

investment_description.value: TradeDepot brings informal retailers and FMCGs onto a single platform that ensures retailers have consistent access to the inventory and financial services required to build their businesses and provides transparency to manufacturers through the entire supply chain. The company offers a wide range of SKUs, working capital and logistics services to these retailers.

investment_update.value: TradeDepot had a relatively flat quarter with sales reaching $38.6m versus $37.7m in Q3 2023, and gross margins remained flat. The company is working towards profitability by Q2 2024, launching initiatives like exporting products to the UK for FX earnings and securing additional exclusive distribution contracts. They are also investing in growth in Ghana. Priorities for Q1 2024 include launching UK operations and kickstarting growth in Ghana. The main challenge is the Nigerian Naira's devaluation, which constrains Gross Margin growth and keeps dollar revenues flat despite naira growth.

investment_description.value: Elephant is a health technology company focused on transforming primary healthcare for the mass low-income market in emerging economies. The company has built and deployed a lightweight cloud-based operating system that connects all parts of the health service – from patient records, to clinical support, stock management, billing, and reporting. Elephant primarily serves government health systems in Kenya and Nigeria, with a pilot in Pakistan.

investment_update.value: Elephant had a mixed quarter. Stakeholder misalignment disrupted contract implementation in Anambra State and Federal Capital Territory Abuja, delaying revenue. However, they signed and began implementing a patient-pay contract in Niger State with full stakeholder alignment. A smaller than targeted capital raise (£1.95m vs. £2.5-£3.0m) meant Elephant needed cash, with existing investor Manta Ray stepping up. The NVAF II investment committee approved up to £400k to unlock additional funding. Key achievements include signing a patient-pay contract in Niger State, closing £500k additional equity from Noso Capital, and progressing sales funnel discussions in Nigeria. Priorities for Q1 2024 include securing £1.2m in equity, achieving patient registration targets in Niger State, making progress in Anambra and FCT Abuja, clearing DFC's investment committee for an £800k soft loan, and launching HealthPay MVP in Kaduna State. Challenges include the capital raise shortfall and stakeholder misalignment in key regions.

investment_description.value: Metro Africa Xpress (MAX) leverages technology to provide on-demand mobility services to customers in West Africa while giving riders and drivers an opportunity to own an asset and have a sustainable livelihood. MAX offers commuters a high quality, affordable, app-based ride-hailing service. The company uses a data-driven screening and onboarding process for its riders giving them access to an increased number of customers and access to an asset finance offering for the motorbikes.

investment_update.value: MAX had a solid quarter, with revenues flat QoQ at $5.6m due to Naira devaluation. They activated more vehicles in Q4 than ever before (4,769), improved collection rates to over 90%, and completed due diligence on M&A target Pikipiki to expand to 5 countries. They also launched operations in Abuja, Nigeria, and onboarded a new CFO and COO for West Africa. They are in due diligence for debt financing with Untapped and AHL. Priorities for Q1 2024 include closing additional debt, progressing the Pikipiki acquisition, and agreeing on a term sheet for the pre-Series C equity round. Challenges include the Naira's devaluation, difficulty securing a lead for the pre-Series C round, and struggles to raise sufficient debt capital for growth.

investment_description.value: Turaco sells simple and affordable insurance products through distribution partnerships with companies who have access to low-income customers, staff, suppliers or distributors at scale and are willing to enable access to insurance to them. Turaco differentiates itself from traditional insurance brokers by specifically designing and selling insurance products for low-income consumers, as well as managing administration and claims on behalf of underwriters. Turaco currently has operations in Kenya, Uganda, Nigeria and Ghana.

investment_update.value: Turaco's Q4 2023 reflected challenges and opportunities. Gross Written Premiums (GWPs) were $615k, 22% below Q3, due to macroeconomic challenges and funding constraints faced by partners. Momentum picked up late in the period with 'Autopay' mobile money payment with Airtel in Uganda. Following a strategy session, Turaco focused on deepening existing partnerships and closing new ones with established corporates, yielding encouraging results with M-KOPA and Safaricom. The company has initial approval for a $5-7m soft loan from DFC, and current runway is 12-15 months, aiming for 18 months to breakeven. Priorities for Q1 2024 include launching new products with M-KOPA, closing the Safaricom partnership, restructuring commercials with Airtel, and piloting AI capabilities for claims processing. Challenges include inefficient Airtel partnership, tapering Tugende partnership, and difficulty gaining traction in Nigeria due to macro-economic conditions.

investment_description.value: KOKO is a climate technology company harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across markets in the Global South. KOKO delivers its bioethanol "KOKO Fuel" through over 2,800 KOKO points (fuel ATMs) which are positioned in small shops at the heart of low-income communities. Customers buy the KOKO cookstove and then frequently refill the smart canister for as little as 30 Kenyan Shillings. By switching from charcoal cooking to a sustainable biofuel, each household subscriber on average reduces CO2e emissions by over 5 tonnes per annum, generating carbon credits. KOKO monetises these carbon credits and uses the proceeds to discount the retail price of its cookstove and fuel, driving adoption and undercutting the polluting competition. When applied like this, credits act as a powerful lever to deliver substantial cost savings to everyday consumers and to accelerate the adoption of planet-positive technologies.

investment_update.value: Novastar Ventures Africa Fund II LP invested $5m into KOKO Networks' $46.4m growth equity round (inclusive of convertible notes worth $21m) in Q4 2023. KOKO Networks completes the Fund's portfolio. Since its commercial launch in 2019, KOKO has grown to serve over 1m households in Kenya, reducing CO2e emissions by over 8 million tonnes. In Q4, KOKO launched services in Rwanda, aiming to scale to 1 million Rwandan households in five years. The investment was priced at $525m pre-money. KOKO also achieved a $30m first close of its carbon receivables financing debt facility led by Rand Merchant Bank.

2. MANAGER'S LETTER We are pleased to present the Q4 2023 Quarterly Report for Novastar Ventures East Africa Fund II LP ("the Fund").

# Challenging Market Conditions Our markets continued to mirror the dismal performance of VC globally. 2023 venture funding in Africa was $3.5 billion, falling 46% from last year, with debt comprising 35% of the total.¹ Growth stage investments were hardest hit. There was only one equity deal worth more than $100m announced in 2023 vs. seven such mega-deals the previous year. This isn't surprising since many of the large growth rounds raised in previous years were led by global funds with no dedicated Africa mandates. Those investors largely vanished from the Africa ecosystem to focus on their home markets amidst the broader VC contraction. The absence of large growth rounds limited secondary exit opportunities for earlier-stage investors.

The contraction in funding together with steep rises in cost of living, precipitous local currency devaluations, and company-specific issues drove more than 15 African tech startups out of business during the year, including several that had raised more than $25m of external capital like Dash, 54gene, and Sendy. Other well-known venture-backed firms fought to survive via large layoffs and down rounds (e.g., Copia, MarketForce, and Twiga).

The most vulnerable companies in this difficult environment are those that remain far from operational profitability with an inelastic cost structure. The market has favored capital efficient businesses that don't burn a lot of cash searching for product-market and business-model fit. Companies that need to build vertically integrated business models to reliably deliver their product or service (not unusual in our markets where so many value chains are broken) are finding the current environment extremely challenging.

The silver lining among the dark clouds is best seen by taking a step back and up. Africa VC funding levels in 2023 exceeded those prior to the big surge in 2021-22 by a wide margin (47% higher than 2019 and 69% higher than 2020). Up 14x since 2015, the growth and development of the venture ecosystem in Africa is unambiguous despite the turmoil of the last 18 months.

Africa's fundamental growth story means there is no shortage of large-scale opportunities, and economic downturns have a history of spawning battle-hardened entrepreneurs building businesses that solve real-world problems for everyday people. Companies that weather the storm will be leaner and wiser. They will see opportunities to acquire distressed assets, consolidate their position, access new markets, and grow. Meanwhile, investors committed to the region will find exciting, transformative investment opportunities at better prices.

## Portfolio Performance Many of our portfolio companies have performed remarkably well in such a difficult context. For example: * **Moniepoint** continued to buck the trend, growing revenues by 66% over last year in dollars to $262.9m. In the context of the Naira in near free fall, this is gravity-defying performance. The

That is extraordinary growth into the headwinds of a cost-of-living crisis and slumping local currency in Kenya. * **MAX** activated more vehicles in Q4 than ever before (4,769) while improving collection rates and paved the way for acquisitions that will see it expand from 3 to 5 country markets. * **mPharma** continued to demonstrate remarkable resilience, completing its restructure and pivot to profitability. While management sacrificed revenue growth in the process, gross margins jumped from 28% to 41%, providing a strong platform for sustainable growth to profitability in 2024.

Some in our portfolio have not fared so well in the storm. iProcure's restructure and pivot is unfortunately proving too little and too late. Sadly, that business looks like the first casualty in this Fund. Meanwhile, SureChill and Elephant Healthcare both face short runways to prove their viability, though Elephant seems to have a clearer path to scale if they can prove their patient-pay business model in Q1 this year.

Inevitably, steep FX devaluations in our core markets yielding slower growth in dollars affected all our companies. Slower top line growth and the softening of comparable valuations prompted the following three downward adjustments in the valuations of our holdings: * **Turaco** from $5.9m to $3.5m based primarily on market comparables rather than company performance. * **TradeDepot** from $12.8m to $11m to reflect slower growth in dollar revenues hit by FX and the general economic conditions in Nigeria. * **Ignitia** from $1.4m to $1.1m based on reduced comparable revenue mutliples reflecting the tough current market conditions.

With the two investments made in the quarter, the fair value of the portfolio stands at $125.3m, representing a 1.68x multiple on invested capital (MOIC) and 25% gross IRR (Internal Rate of Return). While we are disappointed to lose iProcure, the Fund has proven to be remarkably resilient in very challenging circumstances.

Portfolio Company Developments The financial and operational performance of each of our portfolio companies is contained in the Portfolio Company pages later in the report. The following paragraphs summarize the most important developments in the portfolio during the quarter.

mPharma mPharma's Q4 was all about rightsizing the business after a hasty internal round was pulled together at the last minute in Q3 to rescue the business. Company-wide layoffs in the end affected over 300 staff, the wholesale and diagnostics business units were shut down and the franchise portfolio rationalized. By December, monthly cash burn had been reduced by $208k to $380k as the company worked towards a 2024 target of positive EBITDA and positive cash flow. Nevertheless, the effects were a 62% decline in Q4 revenues to $4.4m and a 39% decline in the annual revenues to $18.4m (from $30.3m in 2022) - not helped by the Naira's collapse against the US dollar. Despite this, there were significant wins in three of the retail channels where the company's own stores reached EBITDA positive (excluding central costs) for the second consecutive quarter in Nigeria, Kenya, and Zambia on substantially improved gross margins.

M Moniepoint Moniepoint had a strong quarter with revenues reaching $262.9m for FY2023, a 66% increase on FY2022 revenues. The company finalized its microfinance bank CFO hire, bringing in the former CFO of Stanbic Bank Nigeria. In addition, they have made significant steps towards securing a Global CFO with the identified candidate currently in advanced negotiations. These key hires will substantially transform the company's financial leadership, putting the company in its strongest financial position yet. This quarter, the company put on hold its plans to acquire Kopo Kopo as it awaits a decision from the Central Bank of Kenya on its acquisition of a microfinance bank in Kenya. The Series C continues to progress with legals expected to complete by end of February. AfricInvest have joined the consortium with the final round size expected to be north of $150m.
