elephant Elephant had a mixed quarter. Stakeholder misalignment disrupted the implementation of contracts in Anambra State and Federal Capital Territory Abuja causing delays to revenue realization. However, the team banked important lessons from the challenges encountered and, in December, signed and began implementing a patient-pay contract in Niger State after a joint sale to the Governor and the Health Commissioner that ensured full stakeholder alignment. Delayed revenues from Anambra and FCT Abuja plus a smaller than anticipated capital raise (£1.95m versus a targeted £2.5 - £3.0m) meant that Elephant needed cash at the end of December. Existing investor, Manta Ray, stepped up to invest £200k but will not continue to fund the business alone. In January, the NVAF II investment committee approved an investment of up to £400k to unlock an additional £800k from Manta Ray. This capital, together with Elephant's annual R&D tax credit (c. £225k) and a soft loan from DFC now in due diligence (£800k), provides the company with runway to October and time to hit key patient-pay targets in Niger State that will demonstrate that its patient-pay revenue model is scalable.

M max MAX had a solid quarter despite the continued devaluation of the Naira constraining top-line growth; revenues were flat QoQ at $5.6m. The unstable macro-economic environment in Nigeria means it has been hard to secure an external lead for its planned $15-30m pre-Series C equity round. The debt raise has also been lagging although MAX is now in due diligence with Untapped ($10m) and AHL ($10m). Key achievements in the quarter included activating 4,769 new vehicles - the highest number of new quarterly activations all year - and increasing the average collection rate to over 90% after implementing a more flexible approach to restructuring driver contracts which has minimised defaults and optimised retention. MAX also completed due diligence on an M&A target, Pikipiki - an EV bike financing business operating in Togo and Benin - which, if completed, would extend MAX's geographic footprint to five countries.

tu'aco Turaco's Q4 2023 reflected both the challenges and opportunities it faced through the year. The constrained funding environment left many of its startup partners focusing on survival and deprioritizing growth. Tugende, Turaco's largest partner by revenue, was in survival mode with no capacity to drive growth for itself or Turaco. The Airtel partnership has helped Turaco grow its active policies more than 2x from 160k to 415k, but integration has been slow, and Airtel's customers have been difficult to convert to paid subscriptions. With these headwinds compounded by depreciating currencies, Turaco closed out a tough Q4 2023 with Gross Written Premiums (GWPs) of $639k which were 18% below Q3 performance. Turaco still demonstrated resilience by closing FY2023 with growth as GWPs were 13% up from FY 2022 at $2.6m. poa! internet Poa ended 2023 with 39k customers, almost a doubling from the 20k base at the end of 2022 – a remarkable achievement given the cost-of-living squeeze. Indeed, Poa signed up 9,917 new customers during Q4 – representing 25% of their total customer base at the year end, and a 45% increase over the new customers signed up during Q3. This acceleration in Q4 is coming from the new networks now open in Nakuru and Mombasa compounding the growth in the existing networks in Nairobi. Sadly, the doubling of the customer base in the year has not led to a corresponding growth in USD denominated revenues - which rose by 48% from $2.1m in 2022 to $3.2m in 2023. If measured in Kenyan shillings, the revenues grew 80%, during the year. The FX slide has hit Poa particularly badly because all its equipment costs are denominated in hard currency, so even as they make improvements to operations, the lifetime value (LTV) of a customer is going backwards when denominated in USD – from over $80 in 2022 to $56 in 2023. Without the weakening of the Kenyan shilling, LTV would have increased from $80 to over $110 over the last year. The company is working hard to combat the squeeze and have already renegotiated bandwidth prices into Shillings, reduced customer acquisition costs and are working on other cost reductions tied to high density installations in Nairobi. In 2024 they see a path to doubling customer numbers again to 80k and becoming EBITDA positive in Kenya, launching networks into new cities elsewhere in the country, as well as opening their first network outside the country.

SureChill. Sure Chill had a difficult quarter. Despite a $2.5m equity raise in Q3, the company is running out of cash after the Global Vaccine Alliance (GAVI) delayed its vaccine programme to 2025 causing sales of the company's medical vaccine refrigerator to plummet; Q4 sales fell to just $303k, an 80% drop versus Q3. Existing investors, Gaia Impact Fund and Chroma Impact Investment, are prepared to bridge the company to a new equity investor if a term sheet is secured before then.

We are excited to add KOKO Networks as the 12th company in the NVAF II portfolio. KOKO is a climate technology company harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across markets in the Global South. KOKO delivers its bioethanol "KOKO Fuel" through over 2,800 KOKO points (fuel ATMs) which are positioned in small shops at the heart of low-income communities. Customers buy the KOKO cookstove and then frequently refill the smart canister for as little as 30 Kenyan Shillings. By switching from charcoal cooking to a sustainable biofuel, each household subscriber on average reduces CO2e emissions by over 5 tonnes per annum, generating carbon credits. KOKO monetises these carbon credits and uses the proceeds to discount the retail price of its cookstove and fuel, driving adoption and undercutting the polluting competition. When applied like this, credits act as a powerful lever to deliver substantial cost savings to everyday consumers and to accelerate the adoption of planet-positive technologies.

VI. Portfolio Company Reports - KoKo Cont'd Investment Rationale

KOKO is harnessing the global carbon markets to accelerate the transition from charcoal to clean, bioethanol cooking fuel across markets in the Global South. Over 1 million hectares of African tropical forests are destroyed annually to produce charcoal, leading to 1 billion tons of carbon emissions yearly (2% of global emissions). Meanwhile, 600,000 people die in Africa annually from indoor air pollution-related causes. Due to no convenient and affordable clean cooking fuels, people living in Africa's rapidly growing cities have no choice but to continue using charcoal which is readily available and can be bought in small, affordable quantities.

KOKO delivers clean, safe, affordable bioethanol cooking fuel - "KOKO Fuel" - through an asset-light, scalable and proprietary distribution model. KOKO uses the proceeds from carbon credits to discount the retail price of its cookstove and fuel, driving adoption and undercutting the competing dirty cooking fuels. When applied like this, carbon credits act as a non-government economic subsidy channeled into the hands of everyday consumers in low-income communities. Greg Murray, co-founder and CEO, has been a pioneer in the African carbon space, securing KOKO credits' access to the Korean Emissions Trading Scheme and positioning KOKO to enter other compliance carbon markets.

KOKO's economic model is highly attractive due its recurring carbon revenues, its low cost of goods as governments have agreed to remove taxes on KOKO's hardware and fuel and low FX risk as 70% of KOKO's revenues are in hard currency from the Global North.

Founded in 2014, the founders, Greg Murray and Sagun Saxena, have shown tremendous tenacity to build and scale what is, effectively, a new energy utility without government support or significant investment. The initial years were spent focusing on developing its end-to-end technology and securing critical partnerships to ensure fuel distribution and carbon market entry. In October 2019, KOKO launched commercial operations has since grown to serve over 1.1m households in Kenya, across 12 out of 14 urban centers. The company has recently launched operations in Rwanda. The agreement with the Rwandan Government secures a tax exemption on KOKO hardware and fuel and a government commitment to "corresponding adjustments" on all KOKO carbon credits which will enable them to be sold into compliance markets. KOKO plans to expand to 6 additional countries over the next three years.

## Social Impact KOKO's clean, safe and sustainable cooking solution generates the following social and environmental benefits: 1. Cost savings for low-income households – KOKO sell carbon credits to subsidise the price of cookstoves and fuel. As a result, KOKO's stoves are sold for $12 despite a cost of goods of $60 and its fuel is 50% cheaper than charcoal for the same energy delivered to the pot. To date, KOKO has transferred more than $100m of carbon value to more than 1.1m households in Kenya. 2. CO2e emission reductions as a result of moving away from charcoal (a huge cause of deforestation) and towards a cleaner, more sustainable cooking fuel. 3. No indoor air pollution from cooking which averts respiratory disease and deaths. 4. New, profitable business line for KOKO Point partners, typically female-owned or operated small shops, which sell KOKO cookstoves and fuel in ATMs.

## Exit Strategy KOKO is building a new, pan-African clean energy utility with a rapidly growing customer base, attractive unit economics and a strong, experienced team. Possible exit routes include an IPO, an acquisition by a global energy company (e.g. Shell or Total) or an acquisition by a trading conglomerate (e.g. Itochu, Vitol or Mitsubishi). These global players are under pressure to diversity their revenue sources away from fossil fuels and towards clean energy.
